title: Listening - Part 3: Useful Mobile Apps
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about an app they regularly use. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for using the app. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about an app they regularly use. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for using the app. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about an app they regularly use.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: Until last year I wrote important dates on pieces of paper or simply trusted myself to remember them. That worked badly once college became busier, and I actually missed a dentist's appointment. My sister recommended a calendar app. [19]Now it sends me reminders the evening before and again before I need to leave, so I no longer realise too late that I was supposed to be somewhere.[/19] It isn't exciting technology, but it has removed a surprising amount of stress.||Speaker 2 Man: I enjoy exploring cities on foot, but I’m terrible at reading paper maps. On a recent weekend away, I kept taking wrong turns and spent more time looking for the museum than visiting it. [20]I downloaded an app that works without internet access and shows walking routes, because I want to find sights and cafés confidently when I'm in an unfamiliar city.[/20] I still like wandering into interesting streets, but now that happens by choice rather than because I'm completely lost.||Speaker 3 Woman: The supermarket near me changes its special offers every week, and I used to discover afterwards that I’d paid full price for things discounted somewhere else. I don’t have time to visit three shops just to compare labels. [21]With the app, I can check prices before leaving home and decide where to buy the items on my list for the lowest total cost.[/21] I’m not buying more than before; I’m simply being more careful, which leaves extra money for weekends.||Speaker 4 Man: I started learning Italian because my grandparents came from Italy, and I’d like to speak more when we visit family there. I attend a class once a week, but new words disappeared from my memory between lessons. [22]The app gives me five-minute vocabulary exercises each morning and repeats the words I struggle with until I can remember them easily.[/22] It doesn't replace speaking practice, of course, but I now arrive at class able to use much more of what we learned previously.||Speaker 5 Woman: My job involves sitting at a computer, and at the end of most days I used to realise I had hardly moved at all. I didn't want to join a gym because I knew I wouldn't go regularly. [23]Instead, I use an app that counts my steps and tells me when I’m close to my daily target, which encourages me to take a longer route home or go for a walk at lunchtime.[/23] Competing against my own previous totals is surprisingly motivating.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: Until last year I wrote important dates on pieces of paper or simply trusted myself to remember them. That worked badly once college became busier, and I actually missed a dentist's appointment. My sister recommended a calendar app. Now it sends me reminders the evening before and again before I need to leave, so I no longer realise too late that I was supposed to be somewhere. It isn't exciting technology, but it has removed a surprising amount of stress.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: I enjoy exploring cities on foot, but I’m terrible at reading paper maps. On a recent weekend away, I kept taking wrong turns and spent more time looking for the museum than visiting it. I downloaded an app that works without internet access and shows walking routes, because I want to find sights and cafés confidently when I'm in an unfamiliar city. I still like wandering into interesting streets, but now that happens by choice rather than because I'm completely lost.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: The supermarket near me changes its special offers every week, and I used to discover afterwards that I’d paid full price for things discounted somewhere else. I don’t have time to visit three shops just to compare labels. With the app, I can check prices before leaving home and decide where to buy the items on my list for the lowest total cost. I’m not buying more than before; I’m simply being more careful, which leaves extra money for weekends.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: I started learning Italian because my grandparents came from Italy, and I’d like to speak more when we visit family there. I attend a class once a week, but new words disappeared from my memory between lessons. The app gives me five-minute vocabulary exercises each morning and repeats the words I struggle with until I can remember them easily. It doesn't replace speaking practice, of course, but I now arrive at class able to use much more of what we learned previously.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: My job involves sitting at a computer, and at the end of most days I used to realise I had hardly moved at all. I didn't want to join a gym because I knew I wouldn't go regularly. Instead, I use an app that counts my steps and tells me when I’m close to my daily target, which encourages me to take a longer route home or go for a walk at lunchtime. Competing against my own previous totals is surprisingly motivating.

options.text: to keep in contact with relatives

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The calendar app gives reminders that prevent the speaker from forgetting appointments (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker uses an offline route app to locate places when visiting unfamiliar cities (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker uses the app to compare prices and reduce the cost of shopping (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker practises Italian vocabulary regularly with the app (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The app tracks steps and motivates the speaker to be more physically active (evidence [23]).
